rulings.Case Title: JUSTIN CHAPLIN VS. CALIFORNIA STATE PERSONNEL BOARD
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Ruling On Verified Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 5, 2018, Line 1, PETITIONER JUSTIN CHAPLIN'S Motion For Ruling On Verified Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus. Petitioners Justin Chaplin, James Michels and Frank Schonig's petition for writ of administrative mandamus is denied. The precedential decisions of the California State Personnel Board on issues of general application within Board's constitutional authority are entitled to considerable deference per Yamaha Corp. of America v. State Bd. of Equalization (1998) 19 Cal.4th 1, 7. The double jeopardy rule invoked by petitioners is based on the Board's own precedential decisions and is not grounded in a constitutional or statutory provision. While petitioners' legal position is not unreasonable and is supported by considerations of fairness and public policy, the Board's position is neither a clearly erroneous nor arbitrary application of its precedents. The Board's determination that a withdrawal of an adverse action accompanied by backpay wipes the state clean and an employer is authorized to issue a second notice of adverse action based on substantially the same misconduct within the applicable limitations period and with the benefits of a new Skelly hearing without violating its own "double jeopardy" rule that an employee may not be disciplined twice for the same conduct is reasonably based on its own precedential decisions. (P.C. and S.W. (2006) SPB Dec. No. 06-01; Landeros (2004) SPB Dec.04-04; E.W. (1999) SPB Dec. No. 99-09). The Board's double jeopardy precedents are not based on Government Code 19575. The Board's determination that a second notice of adverse action based on substantially the same conduct as an earlier notice may be issued after discipline has become final on the first notice and then withdrawn does not run afoul of either section 19575 or any other statute or constitutional provision that has been cited by petitioners. Had the Legislature intended to bar the issuance of a second notice of adverse action after discipline based on the first notice became effective, it could have said so explicitly. There is no indication of any such intent in any of the statutes establishing the Board's procedures. While this court is sympathetic to the fairness concerns underlying the petition, fidelity to administrative law precedents and the limited scope of this court's review precludes the granting of the petition. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for respondents is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: POLK STREET GROUP LLC VS. BITTERS BOCK & RYE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Further Responses
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, June 5, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF POLK STREET GROUP LLC's Motion For Order Compelling Further Responses. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses is denied. While the 45-day limitation for filing a motion to compel after service of verified response for filing is not jurisdictional, it renders the court without authority to rule on motions to compel other than to deny them. (Sexton v. Superior Court of Los Angeles County, 58 Cal. App. 4th 1403.) The request for sanctions is denied. Hearing required for the Court to address the Defendant's request for recusal of the Judge Pro Tem. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: POLK STREET GROUP LLC VS. BITTERS BOCK & RYE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Relief For Untimely Motion To Compel
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, June 5, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF POLK STREET GROUP LLC's Motion For Relief For Untimely Motion To Compel. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's request for C.C.P. 473 relief is not available for failure to file a document request discovery motion within the 45 days mandated by C.C.P. 2031.310. Weil and Brown Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2009) §8:1150, citing Zellerino v. Brown (1991) 235 Cal. App. 3d 1097, does suggest relief may be obtained under C.C.P. Section 473. However the Court of Appeal held in that case C.C.P. § 473(b) relief can only be obtained when the Discovery Act does not provide a remedy. Zellerino involved expert disclosure and none of the cases citing Zellerino involved the 45 day rule. There has been no reported case where C.C.P. 473 relief was granted when the 45 day rule applied. The request for sanctions is denied. Hearing required for the Court to address the Defendant's request for recusal of the Judge Pro Tem. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THEDA FUNG VS. SUSANA MANCIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Responses Striking Objection Made By Theda Fung
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, June 5, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT TERRA NOVA REAL ESTATE SERVICES, INC.'s, Motion To Compel Responses Striking Objection Made By Theda Fung. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' motion to compel responses to Request for Documents and Special Interrogatories by Plaintiff and striking Plaintiff's stated objections to same is granted. The responses served were not timely which resulted in objections being waived. Plaintiff's letter agreeing to waiver of objections is insufficient. The plaintiff shall provide full and complete formal verified responses without objection to the Request for Documents and Special Interrogatories within twenty days of service of a copy of the Order herein with Notice of Entry. Monetary sanctions in the sum of $1500.00 are awarded to the Defendant as against Plaintiff to be paid within the aforesaid twenty days. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
